Cardinal Truth

Every claim tells a story. Cardinal Truth is where the story gets straight — the code, the regulation, the field evidence, the paper trail. Adjusters read what's in the file. Make sure the file says what you mean.

Resource Library
 

Resource Library

Two hubs. Insurance is everything you need working a claim. General is codes, materials, and manufacturer specs. Pick a side.

Cardinal Roofing & Renovations, LLC — 5735 Webster St, Dayton OH 45414 · 937.576.6753 · Admin@cardinalrenovations.net

Resource Library v3 · July 2026 · Sources: 2019 Residential Code of Ohio, 2024 Ohio Building Code, Ohio Admin. Code Title 4101:8, Ohio Admin. Code 3901-1-54, Ohio Revised Code Chapters 3901, 3951, 1345. Not legal advice.

Insurance

Working a claim — from the code you cite to the way you document the file to the way you keep yourself on the right side of ORC Ch. 3951.

General

The construction side. Code is what the roof has to meet. Manufacturer specs are what the warranty demands. Both matter on every job.

Ohio Insurance Law

How Ohio regulates the way carriers must handle roof claims. Insurance-side citations govern the claim process; construction-code citations govern the roof itself. Both matter.

Ohio Matching Regulation

OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b) Insurance side

When an interior or exterior loss requires replacement of an item and the replaced item does not match the quality, color or size of the item suffering the loss, the insurer shall replace as much of the item as necessary to result in a reasonably comparable appearance.

Use it when: An adjuster is offering to replace only the damaged slope or a patch of shingles, and the discontinued shingle, faded field, or line-of-sight mismatch means the repair will not blend. Frame the argument around reasonably comparable appearance. Document with dated photos of the field vs. any test shingles from the same lot.
What limits it: Wright v. State Farm (6th Cir. 2014) held that this rule does not automatically require full-roof replacement for any mismatch. The insured must show the proposed repair would not produce a reasonably comparable appearance — not just that a mismatch exists.
partial replacementdiscontinued shingleline-of-sight

Unfair Claims Settlement Practices

ORC Chapter 3901.20 OAC 3901-1-54 (whole rule) Insurance side

Ohio prohibits carriers from misrepresenting facts or policy provisions, failing to acknowledge or act reasonably promptly on communications, refusing to pay claims without conducting a reasonable investigation, or compelling insureds to litigate to recover amounts due under the policy.

Use it when: An adjuster ignores your supplement, denies without inspecting, or delays past what's reasonable. Document every contact — date, name, what was said. A pattern of delay or non-response is the foundation for a bad-faith claim later.
Practical: Send every meaningful communication in writing (email or portal message). Verbal claims of denial are hard to enforce later; written ones are not.
bad faithdelaydenialdocumentation

Public Adjuster Licensing

ORC Chapter 3951 Insurance side

Public adjusters represent the policyholder, not the carrier, and must be licensed in Ohio. A roofer is not a public adjuster and cannot legally negotiate scope or price on the homeowner's behalf. That is the line: roofers document damage and provide their own estimate; public adjusters or attorneys negotiate with the carrier.

Use it when: A claim is stuck and you're being asked to negotiate. Refer the homeowner to a licensed public adjuster or attorney — both to protect them and to protect Cardinal from an unlicensed-practice complaint. Providing your written scope is fine. Arguing for coverage is not.
scopenegotiationreferral

Home Solicitation 3-Day Right of Rescission

ORC §1345.23(B)(2) Contract side

Contracts signed at the buyer's home for goods or services over $25 include a mandatory 3 business day right to cancel. Cardinal's roofing, siding, and window contracts include the ORC-compliant notice on the signature page and duplicate copies attached.

Use it when: Reminding a homeowner that the 3-day window has started; explaining why work cannot begin until day 4 unless the homeowner waives the right in a genuine emergency (rare and narrow).
contractcancellation

Construction Codes

Residential work is governed by the 2019 Residential Code of Ohio (RCO), based on the 2018 IRC. Commercial and multi-family fall under the 2024 Ohio Building Code (OBC). Municipal jurisdictions can amend either.

Residential — RCO Chapter 9

When Roof Recover is Prohibited

RCO R908.3 OAC 4101:8-9-01

A roof recover (layover) is not permitted where any of the following apply:

  • The existing roof or covering is water-soaked or has deteriorated to the point that it is not adequate as a base for additional roofing.
  • The existing covering is slate, clay, cement or asbestos-cement tile.
  • The existing roof has two or more applications of any type of roof covering.
  • Wood shingle or shake without a gypsum/mineral-fiber barrier.
Use it when: Adjuster is proposing a layover on a house with two existing layers, or partial repair on a roof with a deteriorated first layer. Code compels tear-off. This is the single biggest lever for turning a repair scope into a full-replacement scope where Ordinance & Law coverage applies.
Document: Photo the shingle edge showing layer count. Photo any water-staining, blistering, or delamination on the first layer visible from attic or lifted edge. Note visible layer count in the inspection report.
tear-off2+ layersfull replacementordinance & law

Ice Barrier Requirement

RCO R905.1.2

Ohio's Table 301.2(1) designates the entire state as an ice-forming region. An ice barrier is required on new asphalt shingle, metal shingle, mineral-surfaced roll, slate, wood shingle, and wood shake roofs. The barrier must be either two layers of underlayment cemented together, or a self-adhering polymer-modified bitumen sheet. It must extend from the eave edge to a point at least 24 inches inside the exterior wall line of the building, measured along the slope.

On roofs with slope ≥ 8:12, the barrier extends 36 inches up the slope from the eave.

Use it when: An adjuster's scope for a re-roof doesn't include ice & water membrane, or only shows a strip at the eave without the full wall-line coverage. Code requires it. Add the line item and cite R905.1.2.
Document: Note the overhang depth in inches (drip edge to exterior wall). This determines how far up the slope the barrier must extend. Include in the measurement worksheet.
ice & watereaveunderlayment

Underlayment Requirements

RCO R905.1.1 RCO Table R905.1.1(2)

Asphalt shingle underlayment must conform to ASTM D226, D4869, or D6757 (synthetic). Application depends on slope:

  • 2:12 up to < 4:12: Two layers of underlayment required (or self-adhering underlayment throughout).
  • 4:12 and steeper: One layer of underlayment required.
Use it when: Adjuster's scope shows single-layer felt on a low-slope section. Code requires double-layer or self-adhering for anything under 4:12. Common on porch roofs, dormers, and additions.
feltsyntheticlow slope

Asphalt Shingle Fastening

RCO R905.2.5

Fasteners must be galvanized steel, stainless, aluminum, or copper, minimum 12-gauge shank with a minimum 3/8-inch diameter head. Length must penetrate through the roofing material and at least 3/4 inch into the sheathing (or fully through sheathing less than 3/4 inch thick).

Minimum count: 4 nails per strip shingle in standard wind zones; 6 nails in high-wind zones (roof slope > 21:12 or where manufacturer requires it). Manufacturer's instructions may require more — the code minimum is a floor, not a ceiling.

Use it when: Documenting installation failures on the existing roof (nail placement above the seal strip, exposed nails, blow-off patterns from under-nailing). Also for defending your own scope when an adjuster tries to strip out a "high-wind installation" line item.
Document: Close-up photo of exposed nail failure, blown-off shingles showing nail count and placement. Note manufacturer of existing shingle if identifiable; call their tech line for the nailing spec.
nailswind damageinstallation defect

Drip Edge

RCO R905.2.8.5

Drip edge is required at eaves and rakes of shingle roofs. It must be corrosion-resistant metal, extend at least 1/4 inch below the sheathing, and extend back onto the roof deck a minimum of 2 inches. Adjacent segments overlap at least 2 inches. Fasten at intervals not exceeding 16 inches on center. At eaves, underlayment is installed over the drip edge; at rakes, underlayment is installed under the drip edge.

Use it when: Adjuster's scope excludes new drip edge on a full re-roof. Code requires it on every eave and rake. Legacy roofs installed before drip edge was required often lack it entirely — document that and add it as required-by-code.
drip edgeeaverake

Flashings — Step, Wall, Valley, Chimney

RCO R905.2.8 RCO R903.2

Base, cap, and step flashing must be corrosion-resistant metal, sized and installed to prevent water intrusion at wall intersections, chimneys, skylights, and roof penetrations. Step flashing at sidewalls must be a minimum of 4 inches high and 4 inches wide, integrated shingle-by-shingle, and covered by the wall covering above (siding, brick, or counter-flashing at masonry).

Use it when: Adjuster is proposing to reuse existing flashings on a re-roof, or excludes chimney counter-flashing. Reuse is functionally impossible without damaging the flashing during shingle removal; code demands new integrated flashings on new installations.
Document: Photo the existing step flashing condition (rusted, sealed with caulk, short-length, or missing entirely). Note whether counter-flashing is cut into masonry or surface-sealed.
step flashingcounter flashingchimneyreuse

Attic Ventilation

RCO R806

Enclosed attics and rafter spaces must have cross-ventilation. Minimum net free ventilation area is 1/150 of the ventilated space, reducible to 1/300 when at least 40% and not more than 50% of the ventilation is in the upper portion of the space (i.e., balanced ridge + soffit intake).

Use it when: Existing roof shows heat blistering, premature granule loss, or ice damming caused by inadequate ventilation. Re-roof scope should include bringing ventilation to code. Adjusters often exclude ventilation upgrades as "improvement" — the counter is that installing a non-compliant assembly on the new roof would violate code.
Document: Photo existing ventilation type (ridge, box, gable, power). Check for soffit intake — blocked or absent soffit vents are the most common defect. Note attic temperature vs outdoor if inspecting on a warm day.
ventilationridge ventsoffitice damming

Roof Decking / Sheathing

RCO R803 RCO R908.3

Sheathing must be structurally sound and capable of holding fasteners. Wood structural panels (OSB, plywood) must meet the span rating for the framing. Spaced or plank sheathing is acceptable on many older homes but limits fastener options and may require overlay. Deteriorated, delaminated, or rotted decking must be replaced before installing new roof covering — a re-cover over unsound decking is prohibited.

Use it when: Discovering rot or delamination during tear-off. Photograph before covering. Ohio code makes decking replacement a code-required scope item when the existing sheathing cannot support new roofing; Ordinance & Law coverage often pays for this even when standard coverage doesn't. Verify the homeowner's policy has O&L coverage before promising anything.
Document: Photo each section of failed decking with a tape measure or ruler in frame for scale. Log square footage replaced. Save the removed board as evidence when reasonable.
deckingrotordinance & law
Commercial — OBC Chapter 15

2024 Ohio Building Code — Chapter 15

OBC Chapter 15 OAC 4101:1

Commercial and multi-family structures fall under the Ohio Building Code (adopted from IBC 2021), administered by the Ohio Board of Building Standards. Chapter 15 (Roof Assemblies and Rooftop Structures) parallels the residential Chapter 9 but with heavier structural, drainage, and fire-resistance requirements.

Key differences from residential:

  • Class A/B/C roof covering ratings mandatory (UL 790 or ASTM E108).
  • Wind uplift resistance per ASCE 7 (FM 4470, UL 580, or Miami-Dade approval).
  • Overflow scupper or secondary drainage required.
  • Minimum 1/4:12 positive drainage on low-slope assemblies (with reroof exception per OBC 1511).
Use it when: Estimating on a commercial property (mixed-use, apartments over 3 units, retail, industrial). Insurance claims on commercial roofs go through commercial adjusters who expect commercial-code compliance and commercial documentation standards (RS Means, formal moisture surveys, roof condition reports).

Commercial Reroofing Rules

OBC 1511

Similar to residential R908, but applies to modified bitumen, TPO, EPDM, PVC, built-up, and metal panel systems. Recover is prohibited where the existing membrane is water-soaked, has two or more roof assemblies present, or where the existing insulation cannot be dried. Slate, clay, and cement tile also prohibit recover.

Use it when: Documenting flat-roof claims on light commercial or multi-family properties. Moisture surveys (infrared or capacitance) become the standard evidence rather than shingle test squares.
Miami Valley — Municipal Amendments

City of Dayton

Dayton enforces the 2019 Residential Code of Ohio through the Division of Building Services. Roof replacement typically does not require a permit if it does not involve structural work, alteration of the roof geometry, or replacement of more than 50% of the sheathing. Structural repairs, additions, or full deck replacement do require a permit.

Check with: Dayton Division of Building Services for the specific project. Local enforcement practice varies, especially in older neighborhoods where legacy structure is common. Historic-district properties may have additional review requirements.

City of Kettering

Kettering's Building Department distinguishes four re-roofing tiers, with permit and inspection required only for total replacement (defined as tear-off with more than 50% of sheathing replacement). Tear-offs without significant deck work do not require a permit but must still comply with the RCO. Applications and inspections are handled through the online portal.

tier systemonline portal

Huber Heights

Huber Heights enforces the 2019 RCO. Roofing permits are required for full replacements with structural work; simple tear-off/recover generally does not require a permit but must be code-compliant. Reach out to the Huber Heights Division of Development for project-specific determination.

Other Miami Valley Jurisdictions

Beavercreek, Centerville, Springboro, Moraine, Trotwood, West Carrollton, and Vandalia all enforce the RCO with similar local variance around permit thresholds. As a rule for the region:

  • Simple tear-off and re-cover with matching materials generally does not require a permit.
  • Any structural work — rafters, trusses, dormers, deck replacement over 50% — requires a permit.
  • Historic-district or overlay-zoned properties require review before work begins.
Practice: Call the AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) before signing the contract if there's any doubt. A denied permit mid-job costs more than a five-minute phone call.

Policy Provisions

Three coverages that turn a partial claim into a full one. Check the declarations page and endorsements before making any promises.

Ordinance & Law Coverage

Often listed as Coverage D or Increased Cost of Construction on HO-3 policies. Pays for costs incurred to bring the property into current code compliance as part of a covered loss. Without it, the carrier only owes the pre-loss condition — code-required upgrades come out of the homeowner's pocket.

Check for: percentage of Coverage A dwelling amount (10%, 25%, 50%), or a flat dollar limit. Some policies exclude it entirely, some include it automatically. If absent, decking replacement and ventilation upgrades required by code become the homeowner's cost.

RCV vs ACV Settlement

Replacement Cost Value: carrier pays the full cost to replace with like kind and quality, minus deductible. Depreciation is initially withheld and released upon completion of the work.

Actual Cash Value: carrier pays RCV minus depreciation. Depreciation is not recoverable.

Common trap: A roof-age endorsement can convert an otherwise-RCV policy to ACV on the roof specifically after a certain roof age (often 15 or 20 years). Homeowner may not know this. Read the endorsement pages, not just the declarations.

Matching Endorsement

Some carriers offer an optional endorsement that expands the state matching regulation and explicitly requires uniform-appearance repairs including full replacement of undamaged sections when necessary. Others explicitly limit matching to a single slope or single side of a wall.

Check for: "matching," "uniform appearance," "cosmetic" language in the policy schedule and any endorsements. Some carriers add a cosmetic-damage exclusion that specifically strips matching for hail spatter without functional damage.

Documentation Checklist

Photos, notes, and paper that make or break a claim. The adjuster reads what's in the file — make sure the file says what you mean.

Per-Inspection Documentation Standard

Every insurance inspection should include:

  • Ground shots of all elevations — establishes property identity, general condition.
  • Shingle layer count — edge photo showing visible layers.
  • Test square — chalked 10x10 for hail, hit count per slope.
  • Damage close-ups — ruler or coin in frame for scale.
  • Ventilation type & condition — ridge, box, gable, power. Soffit intake status.
  • Flashing condition — step, wall, chimney, skylight, pipe boots.
  • Gutter line — downspouts for granule accumulation.
  • Attic access — decking from below, insulation depth, ventilation from inside, active leaks.
  • Related property damage — siding, screens, HVAC, gutters, fascia, soffit, downspouts. Photograph even if not this claim's scope.
  • Date & time stamp on all photos.

Photo Templates by Damage Type

Hail:

  • One overall of each slope from ground.
  • One chalked 10×10 test square per slope with hit count written on chalk or paper card in frame.
  • Close-up of representative impact showing fractured mat, granule displacement, and matted circular pattern — ruler in frame for scale.
  • Ridge/hip cap close-up if impacted — hail rounds often show best on ridges.
  • Soft metals for corroboration: gutter, downspout elbow, vent hood, cap flashing, HVAC fins, screens, garage door.

Wind:

  • Missing shingle areas photographed with something for scale.
  • Lifted/creased shingles — from the side showing the crease line.
  • Seal strip failure — underside of lifted shingle showing broken seal.
  • Ridge cap displacement patterns.
  • Overall pattern shot showing directional damage consistent with wind vector.

Water intrusion (from a storm event):

  • Interior ceiling staining with dated photo.
  • Attic sheathing staining, streak marks, mold.
  • Insulation compression at leak locations.
  • Corresponding exterior condition — failed flashing, missing shingle, damaged boot.
Use it when: Building a claim file for supplement or appraisal. Adjusters weigh volume and pattern; a scattershot of impacts across slopes tells a very different story than three lonely photos.

Paper Trail

  • Copy of policy declarations page — note coverage limits, deductible, RCV vs ACV, Ordinance & Law coverage limit.
  • Sworn statement in proof of loss (if requested).
  • Cardinal's written scope with line-item pricing.
  • Adjuster's scope (Xactimate, Symbility, or PDF).
  • Line-by-line comparison — every discrepancy noted with photo or code cite.
  • Every written communication with the adjuster — email chain, portal messages.

Claim Tips

The most common adjuster shortcuts and the code cite that answers each. Plus patterns we see from specific carriers — not accusations, just field observations to prepare for.

Claim Scenarios

“We'll only pay for the damaged slope”

Adjuster acknowledges wind or hail damage but scopes only the affected slope, leaving the other slopes with the original shingles.

Answer:
  • If shingle line is discontinued or field is significantly weathered, cite OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b) — reasonably comparable appearance.
  • Document color-match failure with photos of a manufacturer sample vs field shingles under matched lighting.
  • If the roof has 2+ layers, cite RCO R908.3 — recover is prohibited, so partial replacement on top of the existing bottom layer is not code-compliant either.

“Just layer new shingles over the existing”

Adjuster proposes an overlay to save money.

Answer:
  • Count the layers. If two or more, cite RCO R908.3 — recover is not permitted.
  • If first layer is water-damaged, cite same section — unsuitable base prohibits recover regardless of layer count.
  • Point out that a code violation is a policy problem for the carrier: the homeowner would be occupying a non-code-compliant structure funded by the claim.

“Decking replacement is not covered”

Deteriorated decking is discovered during tear-off. Adjuster wants to exclude it as maintenance.

Answer:
  • If the policy has Ordinance & Law (Coverage D), deck replacement required by code is covered under that coverage even if not covered by dwelling coverage.
  • Cite RCO R908.3 and R803 — recovering over unsound sheathing is prohibited.
  • Photograph every replaced board with scale, log square footage, submit as supplement with dated photos.
  • Check the policy declarations page before promising O&L coverage — not every homeowner has it.

“A strip of ice & water at the eave is enough”

Adjuster's scope includes ice & water at the drip edge only, not the full 24-inch-past-wall-line coverage.

Answer:
  • Cite RCO R905.1.2 — the barrier must extend from the eave edge to a point at least 24 inches inside the exterior wall line, measured along the slope.
  • Measure the overhang depth: eave-to-wall distance plus 24 inches gives you the required coverage measured up the slope.
  • Slope 8:12 or steeper needs 36 inches up the slope minimum.

“Reuse the existing flashings”

Common on step flashings, chimney counter-flashings, and skylight kits.

Answer:
  • Cite RCO R905.2.8 — flashings must be sized and installed to prevent water intrusion. Reused flashings damaged during tear-off do not satisfy this requirement.
  • Photograph the existing condition to preempt the argument: rust, caulk-sealed joints, missing counter-flashing on masonry, undersized step flashing.
  • New skylight flashing kit is standard scope on any re-roof; the skylight manufacturer's warranty typically requires new flashing with any new roof.

“Ventilation upgrade is a betterment”

Adjuster excludes new ridge vent, soffit intake, or box vent replacement as an improvement not caused by the loss.

Answer:
  • If the existing ventilation is out of compliance with RCO R806 (1/150 or 1/300 balanced), reinstalling the same non-compliant system on a code-triggered re-roof is a code violation.
  • Ordinance & Law coverage applies if the policy includes it.
  • If the existing system was itself damaged by the storm (ridge cap blown off, box vent housing hail-struck), that alone is covered damage regardless of code.

“It's just wear and tear”

Adjuster denies hail damage as normal aging or wear, or claims granule loss is not impact-related.

Answer:
  • Document impact patterns with the HAAG-style test square (a chalked 10-foot square, count of hits per slope).
  • Document circular, offset, or spatter patterns distinguishing hail from mechanical or manufacturing defects.
  • For an aged roof that also has hail impact, granule loss at impact sites (fractured mat under the impact) is diagnostic of storm damage. Photograph in raking light.
  • Brittleness test on a cool day is more reliable than on hot; document date and temperature.
  • Request the carrier's engineer report if damage is denied on that basis.

“Roof is too old for full replacement value”

Adjuster settles on Actual Cash Value only, or applies a roof-age depreciation schedule that reduces the payment significantly.

Answer:
  • Check the policy: does it pay Replacement Cost Value (RCV) with recoverable depreciation, or ACV only? If RCV, the homeowner recovers depreciation upon completion of the work.
  • If ACV only (common on older roofs under some HO-3 endorsements), the recoverable depreciation is not available. Advise the homeowner in writing.
  • Depreciation schedules can be challenged if they exceed the actual condition. A roof rated at 30 years and depreciated at 25 years remaining useful life may be challenged if it was maintained and shows better condition.
Carrier-Specific Patterns

State Farm — Engineer Reports on Denials

Pattern: On disputed hail claims, especially where the initial adjuster acknowledges damage, State Farm frequently orders an independent engineer inspection whose report concludes wear-and-tear or normal aging rather than storm impact.

Prepare for it by:
  • Documenting extensively on the first inspection with test squares, dated raking-light close-ups, and matched-lighting field photos.
  • Reviewing the engineer report for methodology — date of inspection vs. storm date, weather at inspection, whether the engineer actually walked the roof or used photos.
  • Rebuttal via independent engineer or licensed public adjuster on high-value claims.

Allstate — Cosmetic Damage Endorsement

Pattern: Some Allstate HO-3 policies (increasingly, on renewals) include a cosmetic-damage exclusion for hail impact on roofing and metals when the damage does not affect functional performance. Under this endorsement, spatter marks on shingles, dented caps and vents, and minor bruising can be excluded even when clearly caused by hail.

Answer:
  • Read the declarations page carefully — the endorsement is often an add-on that's easy to miss.
  • Document functional impact separately from cosmetic: granule loss, mat exposure, seal-strip failure, reduced service life.
  • If the roof is otherwise near end of life, cosmetic exclusion + age depreciation can gut the claim; advise the homeowner honestly.

Third-Party Administrators & Preferred Networks

Pattern: Some carriers (Erie, Nationwide, others) use third-party administrators or "preferred contractor" networks whose scopes tend to run below fair-market pricing and whose supplements go to a specific approver rather than the original adjuster. Contractors outside the network are asked to match TPA pricing.

Answer:
  • Homeowners are not obligated to use the preferred network. Cardinal's price is Cardinal's price.
  • Document with local material and labor comparables. Regional RS Means, Xactimate published rates, and manufacturer MSRPs are all cite-able.
  • If the TPA declines a supplement without inspection, that's a bad-faith foundation — escalate to the carrier direct.

Aggressive Depreciation Schedules

Pattern: Some carriers apply steep age-based depreciation to roofs at 10–15 years despite the roof being maintained and in serviceable condition. Recoverable-depreciation withholdings can approach 50% of RCV in extreme cases.

Answer:
  • Depreciation is not automatic — it should reflect actual remaining useful life.
  • Photograph the pre-loss condition of the roof to establish the depreciation baseline.
  • Cite the manufacturer's original service-life rating (30-year, 50-year architectural) as a ceiling; carrier can't depreciate faster than the roof's rated life.

Do & Don't

Field practices on the left. Ways this goes sideways on the right. Print or screenshot for the truck.

✅ DO

Read the policy first

Declarations, endorsements, limits, deductibles — before you scope, before you make promises. What's covered on the paper is what matters, not what feels fair.

Document everything on the first visit

Layer count, ventilation, flashings, decking from the attic, damage close-ups with scale, related property. Second trips are how details get missed and how adjusters get a foothold to argue the claim.

Cite specific code sections

“RCO R908.3” lands harder than “code says.” Adjusters respect precision; homeowners trust it. Keep the citations from the Construction Codes page handy in the truck.

Write a defensible scope

Every line item ties back to a code section, documented damage, or a manufacturer requirement. If you can't defend it, don't put it in. A tight, honest scope beats an inflated one on appraisal every time.

Communicate in writing

Email, portal, text with timestamps. Verbal denials and verbal approvals both disappear when the claim gets contested. Written ones do not.

Meet the adjuster on the roof

Being physically present when the adjuster inspects changes the conversation. You can point to the damage; they can't ignore what they've seen. If they show up without you, they're documenting on their terms.

Refer when the claim needs it

Public adjuster or attorney for negotiation over coverage. It protects the homeowner from a bad settlement and it protects Cardinal from unlicensed-practice risk under ORC Ch. 3951.
❌ DON'T

Waive or absorb the deductible

Ohio doesn't have a specific anti-rebating statute, but waiving a homeowner's deductible on an insurance-paid job is an unfair business practice and can be treated as fraud. Some carriers require proof of deductible payment before releasing recoverable depreciation.

Rely on Assignment of Benefits

AOBs transfer the homeowner's right to receive payment to the roofer. Ohio recognizes them but carriers have grown resistant and some policies now include anti-AOB provisions. Get legal advice before adopting AOBs as standard practice.

Inflate the scope

Every line item should be defensible with a code cite, manufacturer requirement, or documented damage. Adding items that don't clear that bar invites the carrier to reject the whole supplement and can invite fraud allegations.

Negotiate the claim yourself

Roofers document damage and provide their own scope. Roofers do not tell the adjuster what the carrier should pay, argue coverage under the policy, or represent the homeowner in settlement. That's public-adjuster or attorney work under ORC Ch. 3951.

Truck Cheat Sheet

One-card summary for the field. Prints on a single page. Take it in the truck.

Cardinal One-Page Field Card

The three levers for full replacement:

  • 2+ layers → RCO R908.3 forbids recover.
  • Deteriorated first layer → RCO R908.3 forbids recover.
  • Shingle discontinued → OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b) requires reasonably comparable appearance.

Code-required scope adjusters try to strip out:

  • Ice barrier 24″ past wall line — RCO R905.1.2
  • New drip edge on all eaves/rakes — RCO R905.2.8.5
  • New step/counter flashing — RCO R905.2.8
  • Ventilation to 1/300 balanced — RCO R806
  • Double underlayment on < 4:12 slope — RCO R905.1.1
  • 4-nail (or 6 in high-wind) fastening — RCO R905.2.5

Ordinance & Law coverage (Coverage D): check the declarations page. Percentage of Coverage A. Pays for code-driven upgrades. If it's not there, decking replacement is on the homeowner.

Documentation minimums: ground of all elevations, layer count at edge, test square per slope, damage close-ups with scale, ventilation type & condition, flashing condition, attic if accessible, dated on every photo.

The line you don't cross: Cardinal documents damage and provides its own scope. Cardinal does not negotiate coverage under the policy. That's public adjuster or attorney work — ORC Chapter 3951.

Manufacturer Guidelines

Manufacturer installation specs are cite-able the same way code is. They set nailing patterns, starter and ridge requirements, ventilation ratios, and do-not-mix rules that govern warranty validity. When an adjuster strips out an accessory line item, the manufacturer's system-warranty spec is often the answer.

📤 PDFs Pending

The specific do-not-mix language, current nailing patterns, and system-warranty accessory requirements for each manufacturer will be populated from the PDFs Theo is uploading. Until then, the cards below capture the general structure — program tiers, product lines, warranty names — but not the authoritative technical spec language you'd cite in a supplement or homeowner conversation.

To fill this in: upload the OC, GAF, and CertainTeed installation guides / do-not-mix documents you have. Each manufacturer's current PDF gets a card with exact quotes, section numbers, and effective dates so the citations are precise.
Cross-Cutting Rules

Class 4 Impact-Resistant — Insurance Premium Discounts

Shingles rated Class 4 under UL 2218 (the steel ball impact test) qualify for premium discounts on homeowner's insurance with most Ohio carriers — typically 10–30% off the wind/hail portion of the policy. The discount is per-carrier and requires the roofer to provide certification of Class 4 rating.

Use it when: Quoting a re-roof after a hail event. If the homeowner's carrier has the discount, upgrading to Class 4 often pays for itself over the depreciation window. Available in all three major lines: OC Duration FLEX, GAF Timberline AS II, CertainTeed Landmark IR.
UL 2218Class 4insurance discount

System Warranty Prerequisites

Every manufacturer's top-tier warranty (Golden Pledge, Platinum Protection, SureStart PLUS) requires that the full accessory kit come from the same manufacturer — starter, underlayment, hip & ridge, leak barrier, and often ventilation. Mixing brands typically drops coverage back to the standard material warranty (which is much weaker on labor).

Use it when: Defending accessory line items in a scope. If the homeowner has a system warranty on the current roof, replacing with mixed components voids that coverage. If Cardinal is offering a system warranty on the new roof, the whole accessory kit is not optional — it's a warranty prerequisite. Adjusters often try to strip starter, ridge, or underlayment lines; this is the answer.
Document: Photo the existing warranty registration paperwork if available. Note which manufacturer's system is being installed in the scope. Save receipts for accessory components as part of the job file.
accessory kitstarterridge capwarranty

Enhanced Fastening / High-Wind Installation

Beyond the RCO minimum of 4 nails per shingle, most manufacturers require 6 nails for high-wind installations and for the enhanced-warranty programs. The nail zone is above the seal strip and clearly marked on modern shingles (LayerLock on GAF, SureNail on OC).

Use it when: Adjuster strips out a "high-wind installation" line item. Answer with (a) the code minimum in the fastening code card, and (b) the manufacturer's requirement for the specific product line being installed. When the shingle is a premium product with an enhanced warranty, 6-nail is not upgrade — it's spec.
6-nailhigh windenhanced warranty
Owens Corning

Product Lines & SureNail

Main lines: TruDefinition Duration (standard architectural), Duration Storm (SBS-modified impact-resistant), Duration FLEX (Class 4 UL 2218). All feature the SureNail reinforced nail zone — a fabric strip that gives the fastener double-thickness engagement and makes the 6-nail installation the manufacturer default for most enhanced warranties.

SureNailDurationClass 4

Contractor Program & Warranty Tiers

Tiers:

  • OC Preferred Contractor — entry level, standard warranty access.
  • OC Platinum Preferred — top tier, invitation-only, approximately 1% of OC contractors. Unlocks the Platinum Protection Limited Warranty, which includes non-prorated system coverage, algae resistance, and wind damage.
  • Top of the House — elite recognition for high-performing Platinum Preferred contractors.

Platinum Protection requires the full Total Protection Roofing System — OC starter, ice & water barrier (WeatherLock or equivalent), underlayment (ProArmor), hip & ridge (ProEdge Hip & Ridge or DecoRidge), and ventilation.

Cardinal status: [to fill in — current OC contractor tier goes here for reference in scopes and homeowner conversations].
Platinum PreferredTotal Protection System

Do-Not-Mix Rules

Awaiting the OC installation guide PDF. Once uploaded, this card will hold the specific mixing prohibitions and the section numbers to cite — e.g., which underlayments cannot be used with which shingle lines, ventilation kit requirements for the Total Protection warranty, and starter-strip substitutions that void the enhanced warranty.

GAF

Product Lines & LayerLock

Main lines: Timberline HDZ (most common architectural, the industry volume leader), Timberline UHDZ (thicker profile, premium look), Timberline AS II (Class 4 impact-resistant). StainGuard Plus algae protection is standard on most lines. The LayerLock reinforced nail zone runs across the nail area and enables straightforward 6-nail installation with wide placement tolerance.

LayerLockStainGuard PlusTimberline

Contractor Program & Warranty Tiers

Tiers:

  • GAF Certified — entry level, offers the System Plus warranty (50-year materials, 10-year workmanship).
  • Gold Elite — mid-tier designation, prerequisite for Master Elite in some markets.
  • Master Elite — top tier, roughly 2–3% of contractors. Requires state licensing, insurance, business tenure, and ongoing training. Unlocks the Golden Pledge warranty — GAF's strongest coverage: 50-year non-prorated materials, 25-year workmanship, and manufacturer inspection.

Silver Pledge and Golden Pledge require the full Lifetime Roofing System: GAF starter (Pro-Start or StarterMatch), leak barrier (WeatherWatch or StormGuard), underlayment (FeltBuster, Deck-Armor, or Tiger Paw), hip & ridge (Seal-A-Ridge, TimberTex, or Ridglass), and attic ventilation.

Cardinal status: [to fill in — current GAF contractor tier goes here].
Master EliteGolden PledgeLifetime System

Do-Not-Mix Rules

Awaiting the GAF Pro Field Guide / Installation Instructions PDF. Once uploaded, this card will hold the specific mixing prohibitions and their section references — underlayment compatibility, ridge system substitutions, and the exact accessory kits that qualify for each Pledge tier.

CertainTeed

Product Lines

Main lines: Landmark (standard architectural), Landmark PRO (mid-tier upgrade), Landmark Premium, Landmark IR (Class 4 impact-resistant). Luxury lines: Grand Manor, Carriage House, Presidential. NailTrak is the reinforced nail zone that gives fastener placement tolerance and supports enhanced-fastening installations.

LandmarkGrand ManorNailTrak

Contractor Program & Warranty Tiers

Tiers:

  • Credentialed Contractor — entry level, verified licensing and insurance.
  • ShingleMaster — at least one crew member has completed CertainTeed's SARP (Shingle Applicator Roofer Program) training. Offers SureStart warranty.
  • SELECT ShingleMaster — top tier, requires two or more SARP-certified crew members and demonstrated CertainTeed installation track record. Unlocks SureStart PLUS — 50-year materials, 25-year workmanship, comprehensive labor coverage.
  • Master Applicator — invitation-only elite tier for contractors installing across multiple CertainTeed product families.

SureStart PLUS requires the full CertainTeed system: DiamondDeck or RoofRunner underlayment, WinterGuard ice & water, SwiftStart starter, MountainRidge or ShadowRidge hip & ridge, and CertainTeed ventilation.

Cardinal status: [to fill in — current CertainTeed contractor tier goes here].
SELECT ShingleMasterSureStart PLUSSARP

Do-Not-Mix Rules

Awaiting the CertainTeed Master Shingle Applicator's Manual PDF. Once uploaded, this card will hold the specific mixing prohibitions and their section references, including any product-line-specific accessory requirements for the SELECT tier warranties.

Other Manufacturers

IKO, Malarkey, Tamko, Atlas

IKO — ROOFPRO contractor program (Contractor, Premium, Elite Premium tiers). Cambridge and Dynasty architectural lines. Nordic (impact-resistant).

Malarkey — Emerald Pro certification. Vista and Legacy architectural lines. Highlander NEX (SBS impact-modified) common in hail markets.

TAMKO — Pro Certified Contractor program. Heritage architectural line.

Atlas — PRO Plus and Signature Select. Pinnacle Pristine and StormMaster.

Use it when: Homeowner asks about alternatives or matching an existing non-Big-3 roof. Cardinal's Big-3 focus doesn't preclude working with these manufacturers, but the warranty tier programs and installation specs need to be checked per project.

Mastering Roof Inspections

Every job on the roof — sales, repair, insurance, warranty — starts with an inspection. What separates a great inspection from a mediocre one is a systematic approach, honest observation, and disciplined documentation. The inspection is where the job is won or lost.

The Inspector's Mindset

A good inspection accomplishes four things at once: an accurate scope of work, defensible documentation, homeowner trust, and (on insurance work) claim leverage. Rushing kills all four. Approach every roof the same way — whether it's a $500 repair or a $30,000 insurance claim — because the discipline is what makes the difference.

The mindset: you're not selling. You're not defending. You're looking. What you find, you photograph and record. What you don't find, you say honestly. Homeowners and adjusters both smell overselling — and both trust an inspector who calls it straight.

Safety First

Never inspect a roof you can't safely walk. No sale is worth a fall.

  • Ladder: extended at least 3 feet above the eave, footed 1 foot back for every 4 feet up. Tie off if unattended.
  • Footwear: soft-soled, non-slip. No boots you'd wear on the ground.
  • Weather: dew, frost, rain, snow, wet leaves all void the day. Reschedule.
  • Slope: 8/12 and steeper needs a rope/harness setup or a ladder-and-camera approach from below.
  • Storm damage: never walk a roof after a hail or wind event without first checking decking integrity from the attic. Storm-damaged roofs can have compromised sheathing that looks fine on top.
  • Storm-damaged trees, live wires, unstable eaves — walk the perimeter first. If it looks wrong, it is.

The Systematic Walk

Same sequence every time so nothing gets missed:

  1. Ground shots — four elevations. Establishes property identity and general condition. Every inspection has these.
  2. Perimeter walk — soft metals first: gutters, downspouts, HVAC fins, garage door, screens. Hail damage shows on soft metals before it shows on shingles.
  3. Ladder up — eave inspection: starter, drip edge, first course of shingles, gutter attachment.
  4. Systematic roof walk — each slope corner to corner, top to bottom. Don't skip slopes because they look OK from the ground.
  5. Ridge and hips — cap condition, ridge vent function, ridge cap displacement patterns.
  6. Penetrations — each chimney, skylight, pipe boot, roof vent, dormer flashing.
  7. Valleys and sidewall flashings — step flashing at wall-to-roof junctions is where a lot of hidden failures live.
  8. Attic (if accessible) — the story from the inside: decking condition, insulation depth, ventilation actually working, active or historical leaks.

What to Look For

Pattern recognition is what turns an inspector into a diagnostician.

  • Wind: creased shingles (crease line visible from the side), lifted seal strips, missing shingles, ridge cap displacement in directional patterns.
  • Hail: circular impacts with granule displacement, fractured mat visible under raking light, soft-metal spatter (garage door, HVAC fins, downspout elbows). Chalked 10×10 test square gives you a hit count.
  • Age: brittleness (snap a granule off — is the mat rigid?), curling, cupping, exposed nail heads, granule loss visible in gutters.
  • Installation defects: nail placement above vs. below the seal strip, 4-nail vs. 6-nail patterns visible on lifted or blown-off shingles, exposed underlayment at edges, missing drip edge.
  • Ventilation problems: heat blistering (small round bubbles in the granule surface from trapped attic heat), missing or blocked soffit intake, non-functional ridge vent.
  • Decking issues: sagging between rafters (rot from below), soft spots underfoot, fastener pops visible in the shingle plane.
Cross-reference: full damage-pattern detail with photo templates lives in the Documentation Checklist under Insurance. This card is the field summary.

Attic & Interior

The attic tells you what the top of the roof can't. Skipping it is the most common inspection shortcut — and the most consequential.

  • Decking from below — look for water staining, delamination, mold, active drip locations.
  • Ventilation actually working — on a warm day, is the attic significantly hotter than outside? Baffles present at soffit intake? Ridge vent visible from below?
  • Insulation depth and condition — compressed or displaced insulation often marks where water has entered.
  • Nails visible from below — if you can see shiny nail points through the decking, they're missing the shingle above and marking areas of high wind vulnerability.
  • Interior ceilings — walk each room. Discolored ceilings, bubbled paint, or water stains on drywall connect to roof issues even when the homeowner says "no leaks."

Documentation Discipline

The inspection is only as good as what you left behind on the phone. If it wasn't photographed, it didn't happen.

  • Overall then close-up for every notable finding. Establishes location, then shows the detail.
  • Scale in the frame — ruler, tape measure, coin, chalk square — on any damage close-up.
  • Date and location tag on every photo (phone location services on).
  • Notes: anything not obvious in photos — homeowner observations, storm date, prior repair history, adjuster comments if present.
  • Attic photos matter as much as roof photos. Adjusters rarely go up there.
  • Related property damage — siding, screens, HVAC, gutters, fascia, soffit. Photograph even when out of Cardinal's trade — those photos strengthen a hail claim.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Skipping the attic because "the ladder's in the truck." Go get it.
  • Inspecting only the damage-side slope when the storm hit multiple slopes. Walk them all.
  • Rushing in bad light — hail damage shows best in raking light, mid-morning or late afternoon. Overhead sun washes it out.
  • Photos without scale. An adjuster can't judge the size of an impact from a photo without a reference object.
  • Verbal-only notes. "The homeowner said the leak is over the master bedroom" needs to be in the file, not just in your head.
  • Skipping the sidewall between a one-story addition and a two-story main house. Step flashing failures live there.
  • Assuming intent. Wear-and-tear damage on an old roof and hail impact can coexist. Document both honestly. Overselling storm damage on an aged roof gets caught — and it damages Cardinal's credibility on every future claim in the neighborhood.

Supplement Templates

Pre-written supplement requests for the denials that come up most often. Each template has (1) when to use it, (2) the specific scope items to add, (3) the documentation to attach, and (4) sample wording you can paste into the supplement and adapt to the specific claim. All citations are Ohio-specific.

How to Use These

A supplement is a formal request to expand the adjuster's approved scope. It goes to the adjuster in writing (email, portal, or Xactimate SP) and needs three things to land:

  • A code or policy citation that explains why the item is required, not optional.
  • Photo evidence of the condition being addressed, taken during Cardinal's inspection.
  • Clear line items with prices that match Xactimate or regional market rates.
Paste, don't parrot. The sample wording is a starting point — adapt it to the specific claim, adjuster, and property. Adjusters recognize a form supplement and discount it. A personalized supplement with the correct citation lands harder.
Code-Required Upgrades

Ice Barrier — Extension to Wall Line

RCO R905.1.2

Adjuster's scope shows ice & water membrane only at the drip edge (usually 3 feet) instead of the code-required extension to 24 inches past the exterior wall line, measured along the slope.

Line items to add:
  • Ice & water shield — additional linear feet to extend coverage to 24″ past exterior wall line (measured along the slope).
  • On slopes ≥ 8:12: minimum 36″ up the slope from the eave.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R905.1.2, ice barrier is required to extend from the eave edge to a point at least 24 inches inside the exterior wall line, measured along the slope. The current scope provides only a 3-foot strip at the eave, which does not meet code. The overhang on this property is [X inches], requiring [Y linear feet] of additional ice & water shield to bring the installation to code. Please add [Y LF] to the approved scope.”
underlaymenteavecode required

Drip Edge — All Eaves and Rakes

RCO R905.2.8.5

Adjuster's scope excludes new drip edge on a full re-roof, or only includes it at eaves and not rakes.

Line items to add:
  • Drip edge — total eave LF plus total rake LF.
  • On homes without existing drip edge: mark as required-by-code addition.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R905.2.8.5, drip edge is required at both eaves and rakes of shingle roofs on all new installations. The current scope [excludes drip edge / includes it only at eaves]. This property has [X LF of eaves] and [Y LF of rakes], and drip edge is required on all [X+Y] linear feet. Please add [X+Y LF] of drip edge to the approved scope.”

Underlayment — Double Layer on Low Slope

RCO R905.1.1

Roof has one or more slopes between 2:12 and 4:12 (porch roofs, dormers, additions) and the adjuster's scope specs single-layer underlayment.

Line items to add:
  • Double-layer underlayment on all slopes under 4:12, or self-adhering underlayment throughout on those slopes.
  • Additional labor for two-layer application.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R905.1.1 and Table R905.1.1(2), asphalt shingle underlayment on slopes of 2:12 up to less than 4:12 must be installed in two layers (or self-adhering underlayment throughout). This property has [describe slopes: porch roof / dormer / addition] at a measured pitch of [X:12], below the 4:12 threshold. Please add double-layer underlayment for [X squares] of low-slope area to the approved scope.”

Attic Ventilation to Code

RCO R806

Existing ventilation is out of compliance (blocked soffit intake, gable-only, single-source, or insufficient net free area) and the adjuster excludes upgrades as “betterment.”

Line items to add:
  • Ridge vent LF (or box vent count) sized for the attic square footage.
  • Soffit vent installation or reconditioning — count of vents or LF of continuous soffit vent.
  • Baffle installation at each rafter bay if required to maintain airflow.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R806, attic ventilation must provide net free area of 1/150 of the ventilated space, reducible to 1/300 when at least 40% and not more than 50% is upper vent (balanced ridge-and-soffit configuration). The existing attic ventilation on this property is [describe: gable only / blocked soffit / undersized ridge], which does not meet the 1/300 balanced requirement. Reinstalling the existing non-compliant system on the new roof would violate code. Please add [ridge vent LF, soffit intake, and baffles] to the approved scope. Ordinance & Law coverage applies if included in the policy.”

Enhanced Fastening / High-Wind Installation

RCO R905.2.5 Mfg Spec

The specified shingle line requires 6-nail installation for its wind warranty, and the adjuster's scope specifies 4-nail (standard).

Line items to add:
  • Enhanced fastening / 6-nail installation labor rate (typically ~15% above standard).
  • Additional fasteners.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R905.2.5, the code minimum is 4 nails per shingle in standard wind zones, with 6-nail installation required in high-wind zones or where manufacturer specifications require it. The specified shingle line, [product name], requires 6-nail installation per manufacturer specification to qualify for the [warranty tier] wind warranty. Please update the approved scope to include enhanced-fastening installation for the full roof area of [X squares].”

Starter Strip & Manufacturer Accessories

Mfg Spec RCO R905.2

Adjuster's scope specifies generic 3-tab starter (or omits starter entirely) instead of the manufacturer-branded starter required for system warranty.

Line items to add:
  • Manufacturer-specific starter strip (e.g., GAF Pro-Start, OC Starter, CertainTeed SwiftStart) — total eave LF.
  • Manufacturer hip & ridge cap — total ridge + hip LF.
  • Manufacturer synthetic underlayment.
Sample wording:
“The system warranty on the specified shingle line requires the full manufacturer accessory kit — starter strip, underlayment, hip & ridge cap, and ice barrier — from the same manufacturer. Mixing accessories voids the enhanced warranty and drops coverage to the standard material warranty. The current scope specifies [generic starter / mixed accessories]. Please update to [manufacturer]-specific accessories to preserve the system warranty the homeowner is paying for.”
Structural — Discovered During Work

Decking Replacement

RCO R803 RCO R908.3 Coverage D

Deteriorated decking discovered during tear-off. Adjuster wants to exclude as “maintenance” or pre-existing.

Line items to add:
  • Deck replacement — square feet of 7/16″ or 1/2″ OSB or plywood matching existing.
  • Fasteners and labor for deck replacement.
  • Dumpster surcharge if additional load.
Documentation required:
  • Photo of each section of failed decking with a ruler or tape measure in frame for scale.
  • Total square footage measurement per replaced section.
  • Photo of the attic view showing the underside of the same area, if accessible.
  • If possible: physical sample of removed board.
Sample wording:
“During tear-off on [date], Cardinal identified [X square feet] of deteriorated roof sheathing that will not hold fasteners and cannot serve as an adequate base for new roofing. Per RCO R908.3, a re-cover over unsound decking is prohibited; per RCO R803, sheathing must be structurally sound. This deck replacement is code-required. If the policy includes Ordinance & Law (Coverage D), this work is covered under that coverage. Please add [X sqft] of deck replacement to the approved scope, priced at [$X per sqft]. Documentation photos with scale are attached.”
discoveredordinance & lawtear-off

Full Tear-Off Required by Code

RCO R908.3

Roof has two or more existing layers, or the existing first layer is deteriorated. Adjuster's scope specifies a recover / overlay.

Line items to add:
  • Full tear-off labor (replacing overlay scope).
  • Additional dumpster / disposal fees.
  • Additional protective measures for landscaping, siding, gutters during tear-off.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R908.3, roof recover (overlay) is not permitted where the existing roof has two or more applications of roof covering, or where the existing covering is water-soaked or deteriorated. This property currently has [X existing layers / a deteriorated first layer, documented in attached photos]. A recover is not code-compliant on this roof. Please update the approved scope from recover to full tear-off, adding [$X for tear-off labor and disposal].”
2+ layersoverlay prohibited
Matching / Slope Replacement

Line-of-Sight Mismatch — Full Slope Replacement

OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b) Ohio Reg

Adjuster approved partial replacement (damaged area only) but the repair will not produce a reasonably comparable appearance due to color mismatch, weathering differences, or line-of-sight visibility with adjacent slopes.

Line items to add:
  • Full slope replacement rather than partial — additional squares to complete the affected slope.
  • Additional labor and disposal for the expanded area.
Documentation required:
  • Photo of an unweathered replacement shingle (test shingle from same lot) placed adjacent to the aged field shingles, taken under matched lighting.
  • Wide-angle photo showing the affected slope in line-of-sight relation to other visible slopes.
  • Manufacturer confirmation that the exact shingle line is discontinued or the color has been reformulated, if applicable.
Sample wording:
“Per OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b), when a covered loss requires replacement and the replaced item will not match the quality, color, or size of the item suffering the loss, the insurer shall replace as much of the item as necessary to result in a reasonably comparable appearance. On this property, a partial replacement of the damaged area will not produce a reasonably comparable appearance because [describe: shingle line is discontinued, color has weathered, damaged area is in direct line-of-sight from the street/adjacent slope]. Attached photos document the mismatch. Please expand the approved scope to full slope replacement, adding [X squares].”
matchingpartial replacement

Discontinued Shingle — Full Roof Replacement

OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b)

The exact shingle line on the property is discontinued and no comparable replacement is available. Adjuster is offering partial or single-slope replacement.

Documentation required:
  • Manufacturer confirmation letter or product-status page showing the shingle line is discontinued.
  • Attempts to source matching product from independent suppliers, with responses.
  • Photo comparison of closest available match placed against the existing field.
Sample wording:
“Per OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b), replacement must produce a reasonably comparable appearance. The existing shingle on this property is [Product Line, Manufacturer, Color], which was discontinued in [year]. Attached is manufacturer confirmation that no comparable replacement is available. Partial or single-slope replacement will result in a visibly mismatched roof with no reasonably comparable appearance possible. Please approve full roof replacement for total [X squares].”

Spatter with Matching Endorsement

OAC 3901-1-54(I)(1)(b) Policy Endorsement

Hail spatter marks are present without functional damage. Policy includes a matching endorsement that expands beyond the state regulation.

Documentation required:
  • Photos of spatter marks across multiple slopes and elevations, wide and close-up.
  • Copy of the matching endorsement from the policy declarations.
Sample wording:
“The policy on this property includes a matching endorsement [reference specific endorsement number if known]. Hail spatter marks are documented across multiple slopes of the roof. Under the matching endorsement, the insurer's obligation to produce uniform appearance requires replacement of the affected slopes even where individual impacts do not affect functional performance. Please approve replacement of the affected slopes totaling [X squares].”
Note: if the policy has a cosmetic damage exclusion, this template does not apply — the exclusion typically strips matching for spatter-only impacts.
Flashings

Step & Counter Flashing

RCO R905.2.8 RCO R903.2

Adjuster's scope excludes new step and counter flashings, or specifies “reuse existing.”

Line items to add:
  • New step flashing at all sidewalls — count of pieces or total LF.
  • New counter flashing at masonry chimneys — LF.
  • Additional labor for cut-in of counter flashing on masonry (if not already surface-mounted).
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R905.2.8, flashings must be corrosion-resistant metal, sized and installed to prevent water intrusion, integrated shingle-by-shingle at sidewalls. Reusing existing step flashing during tear-off is not code-compliant because removal of the existing shingles typically damages the flashing and disrupts the integration with the underlayment. The current scope [excludes new flashing / specifies reuse]. Please add new step flashing at all sidewall intersections and new counter flashing at the chimney(s), totaling [X LF].”
flashingreuse denied

Skylight Flashing Kit

RCO R905.2.8 Mfg Warranty

Adjuster's scope includes new roofing but excludes the manufacturer skylight flashing kit.

Line items to add:
  • Manufacturer skylight flashing kit — one per skylight, sized to the skylight opening.
  • Additional labor for skylight flashing install.
Sample wording:
“The property has [X skylights, manufacturer if known]. The skylight manufacturer's warranty requires new flashing with any new roof; installing new roofing over the existing flashing kit voids the skylight warranty. Additionally, existing flashings are typically damaged during shingle tear-off, making them non-compliant with RCO R905.2.8 water-intrusion requirements. Please add [X skylight flashing kits] to the approved scope.”

Chimney Cricket / Saddle

RCO R903.2.1

Property has a chimney more than 30″ wide (measured perpendicular to the ridge) that lacks a cricket, or has a failed cricket that needs replacement.

Line items to add:
  • Chimney cricket construction — framing, sheathing, flashing.
  • Additional labor.
Sample wording:
“Per RCO R903.2.1, saddles (crickets) are required on the ridge-side of any chimney more than 30 inches wide. The chimney on this property measures [X inches wide] and [has no cricket / has a failed cricket that needs replacement]. Please add cricket construction to the approved scope at [$X].”

Pipe Boot Replacement

RCO R905.2.8

Adjuster's scope includes new roofing but excludes new pipe boots for existing vent stack penetrations.

Line items to add:
  • New pipe boots — count per size (1.5″, 2″, 3″, 4″).
  • Additional labor for boot installation.
Sample wording:
“The property has [X vent stack penetrations of the following sizes: ...]. Existing pipe boots on this roof are [years] old with visible UV degradation and cracked rubber seals (documented in attached photos). Reusing degraded boots on the new roof will produce failures within the warranty period. Per RCO R905.2.8, flashings must be sized and installed to prevent water intrusion. Please add [X new pipe boots] to the approved scope.”
Related Property Damage

Siding & Trim

Same storm event damaged siding, wraps, band boards, or trim. Adjuster's scope covers only the roof.

Documentation required:
  • Wide-angle elevation photos showing distribution of damage across each affected wall.
  • Close-up photos of representative damage with ruler or coin for scale.
  • Test-square photos on each elevation showing hail hit count (mirror the roof test-square methodology).
Sample wording:
“The storm event that damaged the roof also produced damage to the [elevation(s)] of the property. Attached are documented [hail/wind] impacts on the [siding type], including a test-square hit count of [X per 10×10 square]. This damage is contemporaneous with the roof loss and requires replacement of the affected elevations to produce a reasonably comparable appearance. Please add [X squares] of siding replacement to the approved scope, plus associated trim, J-channel, and corner posts.”
sidingrelated damage

Gutters & Downspouts

Gutters and/or downspouts are dented or dislodged from the same storm event. Common with hail (dents in the trough) and wind (dislodged straps and elbows).

Documentation required:
  • Close-up photos of dents / dislodgement.
  • Photos showing distribution around the property.
Sample wording:
“The gutters on this property show documented [hail dents / wind displacement] contemporaneous with the storm event on [date]. Attached photos show the damage distribution across [X elevations]. Please add gutter replacement for [X LF] and downspout replacement for [Y count] to the approved scope. Aluminum coil color to match existing.”

HVAC Condenser Fin Damage

Hail damaged the aluminum fins on the outdoor HVAC condenser. Fin damage reduces heat-transfer efficiency and shortens the compressor's lifespan.

Documentation required:
  • Close-up photo of the condenser fins showing bent/flattened areas.
  • Wide photo showing the unit's location and orientation (to correlate with storm-track direction).
Sample wording:
“The outdoor HVAC condenser on this property has documented hail damage to the coil fins on the [north/south/east/west] side, matching the storm-track direction of the [date] event. Attached photos show the extent of fin damage. HVAC fin repair (combing) or condenser coil replacement is required to restore heat-transfer efficiency. Please add [combing / coil replacement / full condenser replacement per HVAC contractor estimate] to the approved scope, referring to a licensed HVAC contractor for final assessment.”
Note: HVAC repairs generally require a licensed HVAC contractor's assessment. Cardinal documents the damage and refers, rather than pricing the repair directly.

Screens & Window Frames

Window screens are torn, punctured, or dislodged. Aluminum window wraps and cap flashings show hail dents.

Documentation required:
  • Photo of each damaged screen (torn mesh, bent frame).
  • Close-ups of dented aluminum wrap / cap flashings.
Sample wording:
“The property has [X damaged screens] and [Y linear feet of dented aluminum window wrap] contemporaneous with the storm event. Attached photos document each damaged item. Please add screen replacement for [X units] and aluminum wrap replacement for [Y LF] to the approved scope.”

Measurements

Every trade has two ways to measure it: by hand and by report. Both matter. Reports are faster; hands never lie. This section covers both for every trade Cardinal touches.

Roof

Choose your source of measurements. Both matter — and disagreements between them are how discrepancies get caught.

Roof — Hand Measurements

Slow, deliberate, honest. Nothing beats a rep on the roof with a tape and a slope gauge for accuracy on complicated cut-ups.

Perimeter & Facet Basics

Every roof breaks down into facets (flat planes). For each facet, measure the eave length (bottom edge) and rake length (side edge from eave to ridge). The area of a simple rectangular facet is eave × rake. For irregular shapes, break into rectangles and triangles and add them up.

  • Eave: bottom edge, horizontal.
  • Rake: sloped side edge, gable end.
  • Ridge: top edge where two facets meet.
  • Hip: where two facets meet at an outside corner going down.
  • Valley: where two facets meet at an inside corner going down.

Slope / Pitch

Pitch is expressed as rise-over-run in inches per foot. A 6/12 pitch rises 6 inches for every 12 inches of horizontal run.

  • Slope gauge or pitch finder — a rafter square with a level. Set on the roof or against a rake board.
  • Phone level app — iPhone Measure or any level app. Lay against sheathing or shingle plane. Accurate to about ±0.5°.
  • Rafter/joist trick — measure 12″ horizontally from the rake, then measure vertically to the rake board. That vertical is the rise per 12″ run.
Why it matters: Slope determines square footage. A 6/12 roof is about 12% more surface than the footprint; a 12/12 is about 41% more. Same footprint, very different materials.

Squares (100 sqft units)

Roofing is sold and priced by the square — 100 square feet of roof surface. Every calculation ends here.

  • Footprint × slope factor: multiply flat footprint area by the slope multiplier.
    4/12 = 1.054  |  6/12 = 1.118  |  8/12 = 1.202  |  10/12 = 1.302  |  12/12 = 1.414
  • Then divide by 100 to get squares.

Example: 40′ × 30′ footprint at 8/12: (40 × 30) × 1.202 = 1442.4 sq ft ÷ 100 = 14.42 squares.

Waste Factor

Add for cuts, breakage, and starter shingles. Adjust based on complexity:

  • Simple gable, no valleys: 5–7% waste.
  • Standard cut-up (hips, dormers, valleys): 10–12% waste.
  • Complex (multiple dormers, cricket, steep): 15% or more.
  • Metal panels: often less than 5% if pre-cut; more if bending on site.
Underorder: job stops mid-day for material. Overorder: you eat the return trip and restocking fee. Split the difference by knowing the complexity honestly.

Linear Feet — Ridge, Hip, Valley, Edge

Accessories are sold by the linear foot. Measure each:

  • Ridge cap — total ridge LF plus total hip LF (both use ridge cap).
  • Valley — total valley LF (open or closed valley material).
  • Starter — total eave LF (starter shingle runs the full eave).
  • Drip edge — total eave LF plus total rake LF (both edges get drip edge per code).
  • Ice & water barrier — total eave LF × (eave-to-24″-past-wall-line coverage). Not the whole eave — measure up the slope.

Accessory Counts

Walk and count. Don't estimate.

  • Ridge vent LF (or box vent count).
  • Pipe boots (1.5″, 2″, 3″, 4″ — note each size).
  • Bathroom fan / dryer vents.
  • Skylights (measure exterior dimensions; note manufacturer and glass type if visible).
  • Chimney flashings (count sides needing counter-flashing; note if chase or masonry).
  • Solar tubes / dormer flashings.

Roof — Reading Reports

Reports are fast and mostly accurate. But adjusters read them too, so knowing where they go wrong is how you defend your scope.

Hover

Photo-based measurement service. Homeowner or rep uploads phone photos from ground level; Hover reconstructs a 3D model and returns roof + siding measurements. Turnaround typically hours.

  • Trust: total roof area, individual facet areas, ridge/hip/valley/eave/rake linear feet on visible planes.
  • Verify: slope on complex roofs (Hover's slope estimation can miss on multiple pitches), chimneys and dormers (small features get lumped in), roofs behind trees or obstructions.
  • Common issue: back porches / attached structures may be measured as separate roofs; check the diagram.
Field notes to fill in: Cardinal's typical Hover accuracy, when it's failed on our jobs, best-practice photo capture pattern.

Roofr

Roofr aerial reports pull from satellite imagery. Faster and cheaper than EagleView; accuracy is competitive on simple to moderate roofs.

  • Trust: total squares, pitch on primary facets, LF for ridge/hip/valley/eave/rake.
  • Verify: the report's stated waste-factor recommendation is generic — adjust up for your specific cut-up complexity.
  • Watch for: low-slope porches misread as steeper; second-story or hidden facets may be missed if satellite angle is bad.
Field notes to fill in: Cardinal's typical Roofr experience.

EagleView

The industry-standard aerial measurement service. Used by many carriers and adjusters. Reports are highly detailed and generally accepted without dispute.

  • Trust: pretty much everything — total area, per-facet area, pitch, LF for all edge types, drawing accuracy.
  • Verify: ground-level features the aerials can't see: chimney counter-flashing extent, sidewall step flashing, condition of ridge vent, active leaks.
  • Adjuster tie-in: if the adjuster uses EagleView too, both scopes will start from the same measurements — discrepancies then are about materials and coverage, not area.

Other Reports

Manufacturer-branded reports are usually just relabeled EagleView or similar:

  • GAF QuickMeasure — comparable to EagleView, sometimes discounted for GAF-Certified contractors.
  • CertainTeed roof reports — typically third-party sourced.
  • Xactimate sketch — the adjuster's own sketch. Verify slope and per-facet dimensions against your own; discrepancies here are the most common source of scope disputes.

When a Report Seems Wrong

If a Hover, Roofr, or EagleView measurement disagrees with your hand measurements by more than a couple of squares:

  • Recheck your hand measurement — especially slope on the specific facet in question.
  • Look for hidden or missed facets in the report's diagram.
  • For aerial reports, check that all porches and additions were included.
  • Request a re-measurement from the service if the discrepancy is significant.
On insurance jobs: if the adjuster's Xactimate has different measurements than your report, the source that generated Xactimate matters. Ask if it was manual sketch, aerial report, or EagleView-fed.

Siding

Choose your source of measurements.

Siding — Hand Measurements

Elevation by elevation. Nothing shortcuts a wall-by-wall walk.

Wall Area (Squares)

Measure each wall's height and width. Multiply for area. Convert to squares (100 sqft = 1 square).

  • Height: foundation to eave line (or to the top plate for gables + gable triangle separately).
  • Width: corner to corner along the wall.
  • Gable triangles: base × height ÷ 2. Height is peak height above the top plate line.

Deducting Openings

Standard practice: don't deduct openings under 33 sqft (one typical window). Waste factor accounts for cut-around. Larger openings (patio doors, big picture windows) get deducted at full opening size.

  • Standard windows: don't deduct.
  • Patio doors: deduct.
  • Large fixed panels (over 33 sqft): deduct.
  • Garage doors: deduct at full opening size, but add back the header/jamb trim LF.
Insurance nuance: some carriers require every opening be deducted regardless of size. Read the adjuster's scope method before disputing.

Corner Posts, J-Channel, Trim

All sold by the linear foot.

  • Outside corners: corner posts run wall-height. Count corners × wall height.
  • Inside corners: J-channel. Same measurement approach.
  • Window/door surrounds: J-channel around each opening. Perimeter of each opening.
  • Under soffit / above foundation: J-channel or utility trim for the top and bottom of each wall run.
  • Starter strip: bottom of each wall, full width.

Vertical vs. Horizontal Siding

Most siding is horizontal; some accent walls or full elevations get vertical. Measurement is the same for area, but:

  • Vertical: may need horizontal furring strips (extra material). Consider fastening plane.
  • Board & batten: count vertical LF plus battens separately.
  • Shake or shingle siding: more waste (10–15%) than lap siding.

Waste Factor

  • Vinyl lap: 10% on simple walls, 15% on cut-ups with many openings.
  • Fiber cement (Hardie): 10% typical; 15% on complex.
  • Cedar shingle siding: 15% minimum; 20% on cut-ups.
  • Metal panels: 5–10% if pre-cut; higher for site cutting.

Siding — Reading Reports

Hover is the dominant report source for siding. Aerial reports are less useful because they can't see side elevations.

Hover for Siding

Hover reconstructs each elevation from ground photos and returns square footage per elevation with openings marked and deducted.

  • Trust: total siding area, per-elevation area, opening deductions, corner LF, trim LF.
  • Verify: heights on complex gables (Hover's estimation can be off ±1′), band-board detail (may be included in wall area or listed separately).
  • Photo capture: quality depends heavily on how photos are taken — walk the perimeter, keep camera level, include ground-to-eave in each shot.
Field notes to fill in: Cardinal's photo-capture routine for best Hover accuracy.

EagleView / Aerial Reports

Aerial imagery can't measure vertical wall area. EagleView offers a siding add-on that uses street-view imagery, but coverage is limited to visible elevations.

Bottom line: for siding, Hover is generally the better report. Aerial-only is for roof.

Verifying the Report

  • Walk each elevation and confirm the report's numbers within roughly 5%.
  • Compare openings against your walk-through count.
  • Check that band boards, water tables, and detail trim are called out (or not) consistently with what you plan to install.

Windows — Measurements

Windows have two dimensions that matter: the rough opening and the exact frame size. Confusing them is the fastest way to get a wrong window on the truck.

Rough Opening vs. Exact Size

Rough Opening (RO): the framed opening in the wall — stud-to-stud width, header-to-sill height. This is what you cut in new construction and what you'd see with the drywall off.

Exact Size / Frame Size: the outside dimensions of the window unit itself — the actual frame you're installing.

The RO is always larger than the exact size, to allow for shimming, plumbing, and squaring the window. Typical clearance is 1/2″ total width and 1/2″ to 3/4″ total height (extra height leaves room for the sill sloping).

Rule of thumb:
  • Rough Opening = Exact Size + 1/2″ wider + 1/2″ taller (minimum).
  • When ordering a replacement window, you almost always want exact size for the new window — the RO already exists.
  • For new construction or full frame replacement, you specify the rough opening you're framing.

Standard Window Sizes & Notation

Manufacturers use a callout notation: WxxHxx or WWHH in feet-and-inches.

  • 3050 = 3′0″ wide × 5′0″ tall (exact size).
  • 2440 = 2′4″ wide × 4′0″ tall.
  • 3040 = 3′0″ × 4′0″.
  • 2036 = 2′0″ × 3′6″.

Common increments are 2″. Custom sizes are available from most manufacturers with lead time and cost premium (typically 2–3 weeks and 15–30% over standard).

Measuring for a Replacement

For a replacement window (insert or pocket install — where the existing frame stays), measure the exact opening you're inserting into:

  • Width: measure at three points — top, middle, bottom. Use the smallest measurement.
  • Height: measure at three points — left, center, right. Use the smallest measurement.
  • Deduct 1/4″ from smallest measurements. That's your order size.

For a full-frame replacement (removing the existing frame back to the RO), measure the rough opening as if for new construction: stud-to-stud width, header-to-sill height.

Egress Requirements

Under RCO R310, every sleeping room must have at least one egress-compliant window:

  • Minimum net clear opening area: 5.7 sqft (5.0 sqft on grade-level basements).
  • Minimum net clear opening height: 24″.
  • Minimum net clear opening width: 20″.
  • Sill height: maximum 44″ from finished floor.
Watch out: replacing a bedroom egress window with a smaller unit is a code violation. Even if it's just a "like for like" swap, if the old window is undersized (which is common on 1950s and older homes), the replacement must meet current egress requirements or be flagged as a variance issue.

Glass & Frame Specifications

Not measurements but part of the takeoff:

  • U-Factor: insulation rating. Lower is better. 0.30 or below for Ohio's ENERGY STAR zone.
  • SHGC: solar heat gain coefficient. 0.40 or below typical for Ohio.
  • Low-E, double-pane, argon-fill: standard for most quality replacement windows.
  • Frame material: vinyl (most common), fiberglass, aluminum-clad wood, all-wood. Cost and lifespan vary.
  • Grid/muntin pattern: match existing or match architectural style (colonial 6-lite, prairie, etc.).

Doors — Measurements

Same rough-opening-vs-exact confusion as windows, plus handing (which way it swings) is easy to get wrong.

Rough Opening vs. Slab Size vs. Frame Size

Slab size: just the door itself (no frame). Standard slab sizes: 30″, 32″, 34″, 36″ wide × 80″ tall (6′8″). Also 84″ and 96″ heights for tall doors.

Frame size (prehung): outside of the door jamb. Adds 2″ total to width and 1″ to height for a standard 4-9/16″ jamb.

Rough opening: the framed opening. Add another 2″ total width and 2″ height over the frame size to allow shimming.

Quick math for standard 36″ prehung entry door:
  • Slab: 36″ × 80″
  • Frame: 38″ × 81″ (approx)
  • Rough Opening: 38-1/2″ × 82-1/2″ (approx — check the manufacturer's spec)

Prehung vs. Slab Replacement

Prehung: door + jamb + hinges + strike assembled at the factory. What you install into the rough opening. Standard for new construction and full-frame replacement. Cardinal's default for entry door replacements where the existing frame is damaged or rotted.
